results.title: Hepatic concentrations of zinc, copper and manganese in infants with extrahepatic biliary atresia.
results.abstract: The objective of this study was to determine the concentration of Zinc (Zn), Copper (Cu) and Manganese (Mn) in hepatic tissue from extrahepatic biliary atresia (EHBA). Liver biopsy samples were obtained at time of portoenterostomy from 49 infants ages 1.1 to 20.7 months (median 2.1) with EHBA. Samples were dry ashed and analyzed by flame (Zn) or flameless (Cu and Mn) atomic absorption spectrophotometry. Hepatic Cu concentrations are physiologically elevated at birth and decline rapidly during the first 2 month of life, therefore only samples from 29 infants, ages greater than 8 weeks were considered for Cu. Concentrations (mg/kg dry weight, mean and range) were: Zn 142 (70-507), Cu 204 (19-570), Mn 9.1 (2.8-21.8) vs. literature controls in the same age range: Zn 262 (82-543), Cu 92, Mn 4.3 (3.3-11.5). No correlations were found between serum alkaline phosphatase, AST or total bilirubin and hepatic trace element concentrations, between trace element concentrations and age, or between Cu
results.journal: Journal of trace elements in medicine and biology : organ of the Society for Minerals and Trace Elements (GMS)

results.title: Manganese accumulation in the brain: MR imaging.
results.abstract: Manganese (Mn) accumulation in the brain is detected as symmetrical high signal intensity in the globus pallidi on T1-weighted MR images without an abnormal signal on T2-weighted images. In this review, we present several cases of Mn accumulation in the brain due to acquired or congenital diseases of the abdomen including hepatic cirrhosis with a portosystemic shunt, congenital biliary atresia, primary biliary cirrhosis, congenital intrahepatic portosystemic shunt without liver dysfunction, Rendu-Osler-Weber syndrome with a diffuse intrahepatic portosystemic shunt, and patent ductus venosus. Other causes of Mn accumulation in the brain are Mn overload from total parenteral nutrition and welding-related Mn intoxication.
